corridors.label: US -> Mexico
corridors.corridor_note: This is the benchmark corridor. Huge remittance volume. Mature payout expectations. A very fair place to ask whether stablecoin rails are actually better.
corridors.default_current_setup: US funding counterparty + FX corridor counterparty + Mexico payout network
solved_infrastructure: Polygon: low-cost stablecoin settlement makes the competitive case easier to test in a corridor with real payment volume.
Ethereum: trusted treasury and custody path if operator comfort matters more than raw cost.
Stellar: still useful as a payments-native comparator, especially when the conversation turns to remittance rails.
open_questions: Which Mexico payout counterparty gives sample merchant the cleanest operational path from stablecoin settlement into local disbursement?
How much fee improvement is needed to beat mature remittance infrastructure in a corridor this competitive?
What AML and transaction-monitoring evidence would sample merchant require before treating this as demo-ready at scale?

stellar_reference.liquidity_proxy: Relevant payment-first comparator, but the current Canopy build still treats it as reference context rather than a live route.
stellar_reference.settlement: Approx. 5s ledger close; current Canopy view uses batched analytics, not live rail monitoring.
stellar_reference.note: Useful reference for remittance conversations. Still not today's measured winner.

corridors.label: US -> Brazil
corridors.corridor_note: Brazil is the cleanest demo story in the bunch. Real stablecoin activity. Real PIX-adjacent narrative. Real reason to care.
corridors.default_current_setup: SWIFT + FX provider + Brazil payout counterparty
solved_infrastructure: Polygon: Brazil has strong stablecoin-to-PIX narratives and enterprise payment examples.
Ethereum: institutionally familiar rail with broad custody and treasury support.
Stellar: PIX-connected wallet and payout patterns exist, but enterprise route validation still needs diligence.
open_questions: Which local payout or treasury counterparty would sample merchant trust first for production rollout?
How much corridor volume must be executable before the demo is strategically meaningful?
What internal evidence does sample merchant need beyond a live demo to move to a structured demo?

stellar_reference.liquidity_proxy: Good anchor-led story, but still more comparator than live route in this build.
stellar_reference.settlement: Approx. 5s ledger close; current Canopy view uses batched analytics, not live rail monitoring.
stellar_reference.note: Good comparator. Not the live call today.

corridors.label: US -> Nigeria
corridors.corridor_note: Nigeria is the canonical payroll-readiness demo corridor. The story is operational certainty before release, not execution speed in isolation.
corridors.default_current_setup: USD prefunding via treasury bank, local NGN payout counterparty, manual beneficiary review
corridors.launch_readiness: Use with readiness controls, not straight-through release
solved_infrastructure: Polygon: low-cost settlement still matters if the payout stack and control workflow stay disciplined.
Ethereum: higher-trust fallback when operator comfort matters more than transfer cost.
Canopy: decision-layer controls can surface cutoff, beneficiary, and buffer risk before release.
open_questions: Which Nigeria payout counterparty can reliably hold daylight capacity for payroll release windows?
How should sample merchant evidence beneficiary ownership changes before late-day approval?
What release buffer is required when prefunding lands inside the same-day cutoff window?

stellar_reference.liquidity_proxy: Useful payments comparator, but the current build still treats it as reference context rather than live payroll evidence.
stellar_reference.settlement: Approx. 5s ledger close; current Canopy view uses batched analytics, not live rail monitoring.
stellar_reference.note: Relevant committee context, but not the measured release call.

corridors.label: US -> Philippines
corridors.corridor_note: The Philippines is a serious corridor. Big remittance energy. Good payout story. Still needs counterparty diligence.
corridors.default_current_setup: SWIFT + local payout counterparty + treasury prefunding
solved_infrastructure: Polygon: well-understood low-cost payout rail narrative with institutional validators.
Ethereum: broadly trusted infrastructure baseline if cost is secondary to familiarity.
Stellar: strong remittance reputation and payment-native story for committee discussion.
open_questions: What last-mile payout counterparty would sample merchant use to make stablecoin settlement operationally invisible?
How would sample merchant handle attribution and sanctions monitoring across off-ramp counterpartys?
Would sample merchant evaluate this as consumer remittance infrastructure or broader payout infrastructure?

stellar_reference.liquidity_proxy: Good remittance story, but the corridor-grade proof still is not there yet.
stellar_reference.settlement: Approx. 5s ledger close; current Canopy view uses batched analytics, not live rail monitoring.
stellar_reference.note: Relevant context if you like payment-first rails. Still not today's live call.

corridors.label: US -> Vietnam
corridors.corridor_note: Vietnam is interesting. Not ready for chest-thumping. More watchlist than victory lap right now.
corridors.default_current_setup: SWIFT + banking counterparty + manual treasury review
solved_infrastructure: Ethereum: strongest baseline for trust and broad provider compatibility.
Polygon: attractive cost profile and strong stablecoin ecosystem if local payouts can be solved.
Stellar: purpose-built payment story is relevant, but corridor-specific proof still matters.
open_questions: Which regulated off-ramp or payout counterparty is strongest for Vietnam today?
Is stablecoin settlement reducing a real sample merchant pain point here, or only changing the funding leg?
Should this corridor be used for a demo now or only as a strategic watchlist corridor?

stellar_reference.liquidity_proxy: Interesting on paper, but the corridor-specific maturity is still fuzzy.
stellar_reference.settlement: Approx. 5s ledger close; current Canopy view uses batched analytics, not live rail monitoring.
stellar_reference.note: Keep it in the conversation. Do not mistake it for a live recommendation.

corridors.label: Singapore -> Indonesia
corridors.corridor_note: This is Southeast Asia operator reality. Payroll, vendors, regional fintech settlement. Real demand, but infrastructure maturity is not evenly distributed.
corridors.default_current_setup: Singapore treasury account + regional FX desk + Indonesia payout counterparty
solved_infrastructure: Polygon: regional fintech operators will like the cost profile if the last-mile payout stack is dependable.
Ethereum: trusted baseline for operators that care more about institutional familiarity than cheap execution.
Stellar: payments-oriented story is relevant, but corridor-specific operator proof is still thinner than the narrative.
open_questions: Which Indonesia payout or treasury counterparty is mature enough to make regional settlement feel operationally boring?
How much of the corridor read reflects real business settlement versus broader regional crypto activity?
What regulatory and reporting controls would Singapore-based operators need before scaling this corridor confidently?

stellar_reference.liquidity_proxy: Compelling regional-payments comparator, though the corridor-specific operator evidence is still mostly directional.
stellar_reference.settlement: Approx. 5s ledger close; current Canopy view uses batched analytics, not live rail monitoring.
stellar_reference.note: Relevant for SEA settlement conversations. Still reference context in this demo.

corridors.label: Singapore -> Vietnam
corridors.corridor_note: This is a regional treasury-and-payroll corridor where Singapore operating discipline meets Vietnam payout complexity. It is more credible than generic watchlist mode, but still needs real counterparty evidence.
corridors.default_current_setup: Singapore treasury account + regional FX desk + Vietnam payout counterparty with manual release controls
corridors.launch_readiness: Good demo candidate with strong counterparty diligence
solved_infrastructure: Polygon: attractive for regional treasury movement if the Vietnam payout handoff is contractually reliable.
Ethereum: stronger fallback when operator trust, custody familiarity, and reviewability matter more than cost.
Stellar: interesting as a payments-native comparator, though enterprise evidence still trails Polygon and Ethereum.
open_questions: Which Vietnam payout or treasury counterparty can make beneficiary release windows feel operationally boring?
How much of the SG -> Vietnam signal reflects real operating flows versus broader regional stablecoin activity?
What reporting and control evidence would a Singapore-based operator need before scaling this corridor beyond demo mode?

stellar_reference.liquidity_proxy: Useful regional-payments comparator, but enterprise-grade proof is still thinner than Polygon or Ethereum here.
stellar_reference.settlement: Approx. 5s ledger close; current Canopy view uses batched analytics, not live rail monitoring.
stellar_reference.note: Keep it in the comparison set for SEA workflows, not as the primary live recommendation.

corridors.label: Japan -> Singapore
corridors.corridor_note: This is not a remittance story. It is a treasury and regional-operations story between two high-trust jurisdictions with very different infrastructure dynamics.
corridors.default_current_setup: Japan treasury bank + Singapore entity account + regional operating stack
solved_infrastructure: Ethereum: strongest fit when the buyer thinks in treasury controls, custody, and institutional comfort.
Polygon: compelling if cost and transfer speed matter more than blue-chip familiarity.
Stellar: still useful as a payments-native reference, though the corporate treasury case is less proven here.
open_questions: Which treasury workflows in Japan-to-Singapore actually benefit from stablecoin settlement rather than existing banking rails?
How would sample merchant evidence regulatory comfort and internal controls for a treasury-led corridor like this one?
Is the opportunity here operating expense movement, treasury rebalancing, or venture-style capital deployment?

stellar_reference.liquidity_proxy: Interesting as a payment rail reference, but the treasury-grade corridor story remains thinner than Ethereum today.
stellar_reference.settlement: Approx. 5s ledger close; current Canopy view uses batched analytics, not live rail monitoring.
stellar_reference.note: Keep it as a comparator, not as the main live call for this treasury corridor.

corridors.label: US -> South Africa
corridors.corridor_note: South Africa is a treasury and payroll corridor where timing, payout counterparty reliability, and liquidity posture matter more than headline remittance volume.
corridors.default_current_setup: USD prefunding with local ZAR payout counterparty and standard beneficiary controls
solved_infrastructure: Polygon: attractive for lower-fee treasury settlement if payout-counterparty timing remains dependable.
Ethereum: strong institutional fallback when operator trust and capacity confidence matter more than cost.
Stellar: relevant as a payment-first reference, but still more comparator than live decision rail in this build.
open_questions: Which South Africa payout counterparty gives sample merchant the cleanest operational handoff from prefunding to beneficiary release?
How much daytime liquidity needs to be contractually reserved before reducing the release buffer?
What operator controls are required to make ZAR settlement timing defensible during payroll cutoff windows?

stellar_reference.liquidity_proxy: Useful treasury-payments comparator, though the corridor-specific operator proof is still thinner than Polygon or Ethereum.
stellar_reference.settlement: Approx. 5s ledger close; current Canopy view uses batched analytics, not live rail monitoring.
